Feel free to ask more detals if it helps. Here I think it boils down to union semantics, so let me point out the main rules again:
``Union[x, y]`` is subtype of ``z`` iff ``x`` is subtype of ``z`` *and* ``y`` is subtype of ``z``.
``z`` is subtype of ``Union[x, y]`` iff ``z`` is subtype of ``x`` *or* ``z`` is subtype of ``y``.
This directly corresponds to union operation from set theory if x, y, z are sets and subtype means subset. There is also the intersection correspondence in type theory, but PEP 484 does not provide an intersection type (was once discussed in an issue of typing, but then drpped because rare use cases did not outweight the difficulties in implementing it for static type checking/mypy)

To be honest, I don't particularly like it. It looks to me like a Python developer trying to write Javascript.

Generally, the consensus in the Javascript community appears to be to use ``that`` as variable name if you want to refer to the context enclosing a callback (i.e instead of ``self``). This is what Douglas Crockford suggests in "Javascript, the good parts".

In general however, I prefer using things like ``function.prototype.bind`` or ``$.proxy`` to explicitly set the context of ``this`` for callback functions.

Currently the registry supports both patternName and patName.

In Patternslib, patternName is legacy and patName is the newer way. I saw that Mockup uses patternName when I did the merging work at the Alpine sprint.

I personally prefer patName just because it's shorter and the patterns are named like that as well (e.g. "pat-name").

Not a big deal though, can also be renamed if required.

I am open and flexible.  Just want to make sure we are on the same page about the pros and cons.

If I understand correctly, we agree that the class-based approach is (slightly) better; should the need arises, we can adapt to it in spite of inconsistency.

Deduction 1: In the long term, inconsistency will eventually be there anyway, how about start to adapt to the future-proof approach now?

Deduction 2: At a hindsight, if we rewind back to day 1, perhaps we would write the test cases in class-based flavor, so there wouldn't be such inconsistency in the first place. (I suspect that the flatten-function flavor is influenced by the [nose 101 document](http://nose.readthedocs.org/en/latest/writing_tests.html), but 101 document typically focuses only on features, it does not necessarily guide us how to organize the code.)

Last but not the least, if we stick with current flatten-function flavor (which is fine), chances are, we won't bother to refactor old runnable test cases when we need to add more test cases in future because "If it ain't broken, why fix it?", and then even the new test cases are less likely to choose the class-based flavor to violate the convention in the same file.

By the way, in this specific case, both functions use a loader instance independently. We could choose to make that loader instance as a member in this class to be shared by the 2 methods. That should make the test cases run a bit faster. Will that be better reason alone, on top of all above discussion?

-  we dont want to use the corrections until we received all of them.
- There is a common data that would bandwidth if repeated on a single satellite basis

So for the first concern, there are other ways to reach that goal in the filter.  And for the bandwidth concern, I dont think we will send corrections like this to the user in the short term anyway (only used for the network engine interface), as the Skylark user correction message format is far from being defined at the moment.
So I would propose to switch to a static 1 message per satellite correction.

Sorry for not catching this earlier: The problem with `finally:` is that an exception raised there will mask any exception that occurred in `try:`

```
>>> try:
...     raise ValueError()
... finally:
...     raise RuntimeError()
...     
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<input>", line 4, in <module>
RuntimeError
```

There are various ways around this, none of them pretty, caused by the fact that https://docs.python.org/2/library/sys.html#sys.exc_info() is backed by a single thread-local variable.

```
>>> from bd2k.util.exceptions import panic
>>> try:
...     raise ValueError()
... except:
...     with panic():
...         raise RuntimeError()
... else:
...     pass
...     
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<input>", line 5, in <module>
  File "<input>", line 2, in <module>
ValueError
```

The easiest would be to omit the `try: finally:` altogether and accept the fact that on exceptions, permissions might be wrong. But I have the feeling that that won't work for us. bd2k-python-lib's panic() context manager is the next best thing I can think of. It would require that you duplicate the permission fix in both the `except:` (enclosed in `panic()`) and the `else:` (without `panic()`). That can be alleviated by extracting that code into a helper function.

This is not actually the interface. `GettzFunc` is a class representing a callable function. `object` is the name of the base type of all objects in Python 3 ("new style classes"), and so `GettzFunc` is a subclass of `object` for backwards compatibility with Python 2.

I realize it's confusing, but what's being written here is *actually* the documentation for `tz.gettz`, which is an instance of `GettzFunc`, but for practical purposes is equivalent to a `function`.

When you call `tz.gettz()`, it actually invokes [`GettzFunc.__call__`](https://github.com/weatherpattern/dateutil/blob/503b5e9331a4d800bb5875d02612b097c57d12de/dateutil/tz/tz.py#L1501), which itself is a thin wrapper around [`GettzFunc.nocache`](https://github.com/weatherpattern/dateutil/blob/503b5e9331a4d800bb5875d02612b097c57d12de/dateutil/tz/tz.py#L1520).

The reason it's built this way is partially for performance reasons, but mostly because I want `tz.gettz` to satisfy the following invariant:

```python
assert tz.gettz(name) is tz.gettz(name)
```

So I need to store a reference to the arguments that have been passed to `tz.gettz` and all the results, so that I can return the same object if I get the same arguments. Those get stored on the `GettzFunc` instance in the `_cache`. Because you might want the old semantics, I've exposed a "nocache" [variant](https://github.com/python-variants/variants) that bypasses the cache: `tz.gettz.nocache(name)`, and since cache misses are identical to calls to `nocache`, `__call__` is implemented in terms of `nocache`.

There are also functions on `gettz` for manipulating the cache, like `clear_cache`.

No, a proxy object should *never* have a `_proxyObject` attribute. A proxy should just point to its implementation. Nothing should ever add one and if it does then it's wrong.

Better to pass some SummaryWriter-esque object rather than a logdir, since the SummaryWriter is the canonical point by which the summary data is exported.

Reasons:
1. One often keeps access to a SummaryWriter when writing code that deals with summaries, but the logdir might be in some outer scope
2. In the future, we may switch to e.g a DatabseWriter or RemoteServerWriter and in these cases there isn't meaningfully a "logdir". It would be better to implement a consistent interface across all our writers that can handle this, rather than privilging the logdir concept.

1. Written defensively
2. Limit unintended/unexpected side effects on the system.

Whether or not `typing` is packaged by distros is relevant because when people install Certbot through their OS package manager (which is still the recommended way to get Certbot on most systems), `pip` isn't used and unfortunately `typing` isn't widely packaged.

I agree there's a strong argument that playing with conditional dependencies might not be worth it. I think the biggest appeal is that systems that are using Python 3.5+ don't need to install or package `typing` when it's a noop package which could make some of this easier for us.
